verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This Moses whom they refused, saying, Who made thee a ruler and a judge over us? the same did God send to be a ruler and a deliverer by the hand of the Angel which appeared to him in the bush.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is very suitable to the matter in hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This Moses,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This,” the man who had been in danger of losing his life; the man who had been set at naught by them; “this” the man whom they had declined: “this” same, God having raised up, sent unto them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom they refused, saying, Who made thee a ruler?” just as they themselves (the hearers) said, “We have no king, but Cæsar.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He here shows also, that what was then done, was done by Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The same did God send by the hand of the Angel,” who said unto him, “I am the God of Abraham.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This” same Moses, he says,—and observe how he points to his renown—“this” same Moses, he says, “brought them out, after that he had showed wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, and in the Red sea, and in the wilderness forty years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is that Moses, which said unto the children of Israel, A prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36, 37): set at naught like me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Him, likewise, Herod wished to kill, and in Egypt He found preservation just as it was with the former, even when He was a babe, He was aimed at for destruction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This is he, that was in the Church in the wilderness with the Angel which spake to him in the mount Sina, and with our fathers: who received the lively oracles to give unto us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again no mention of temple, none of sacrifice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With the Angel,” it says, “he received the lively oracles to give unto the fathers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows, that he not only wrought miracles, but also gave a law, as Christ did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as Christ first works miracles, and then legislates: so did Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they did not hear him, keeping their disobedience, even after the miracles: “To whom,” he says, “our fathers would not obey:” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39) after the wonders done in those forty years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And not only so, but just the contrary: “but thrust him from them, and in their hearts turned back again into Egypt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Saying unto Aaron, Make us gods to go before us; for as for this Moses, which brought us out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they made a calf in those days, and offered sacrifice unto the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their own hands.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then God turned, and gave them up to worship the host of heaven; as it is written in the book of the Prophets, O ye house of Israel, have ye offered to me slain beasts and sacrifices by the space of forty years in the wilderness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yea, ye took up the tabernacle of Moloch, and the star of your god Remphan, figures which ye made to worship them: and I will carry you away beyond Babylon.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The expression, “gave them up,” means, He suffered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Our fathers had the tabernacle of witness in the wilderness, as he had appointed, speaking unto Moses, that he should make it according to the fashion he had seen.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even when there was a Tabernacle, yet there were no sacrifices.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Did ye offer unto Me slain beasts and sacrifices?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There was “the tabernacle of witness,” and yet it profited them nothing, but they were consumed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But neither before, nor afterwards, did the miracles profit them aught.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which also, our fathers that came after brought in.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou, how the holy place is there wherever God may be?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to this end also he says, “in the wilderness,” to compare place with place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the benefit (conferred upon them): And our fathers that came after brought it in with Jesus into the possession of the Gentiles, whom God drave out before the face of our fathers, unto the days of David; who found favor before God, and desired to find a tabernacle for the God of Jacob. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: David “desired to find favor:” and he builded not, he, the wonderful, the great; but the castaway, Solomon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Solomon,” it says, “built Him an house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Howbeit the Most High dwelleth not in (places) made with hands.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was shown indeed already by what had been before said: but it is shown also by the voice of a prophet; “What house will ye build for Me? saith the Lord God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As saith the prophet, Heaven is my throne, and earth is my footstool: what house will ye build for me? saith the Lord: or what is the place of my rest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hath not my hand made all these things?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Marvel not, he says, if they on whom Christ confers His benefits refuse His kingdom, seeing in the case of Moses it was just the same.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He brought them out;” and rescued them not in a general way, but also while they were in the wilderness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wonders and signs,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that they themselves (Stephen’s hearers) are concerned in those old miracles also?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This is that Moses:” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 37) he, that conversed with God; he, that had been saved out of situations so strange and wonderful; he, that wrought so great works, and had so great power. [“Which said unto the children of Israel, A prophet,” etc.] He shows, that the prophecy must by all means be fulfilled, and that Moses is not opposed to Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This is he that was in the Church in the wilderness, and, that said unto the children of Israel.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that thence comes the root, and that “salvation is from the Jews?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With the Angel,” it says, “which spake unto him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, again he affirms that it was He (Christ) that gave the Law, seeing Moses was with “Him” in the Church in the wilderness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And here he puts them in mind of a great marvel, of the things done in the Mount: “Who received living oracles to give unto us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On all occasions Moses is wonderful, and (so) when need was to legislate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means the expression, “Living oracles” (λόγια)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those, whereof the end was shown by words (διὰ λόγων): in other words, he means the prophecies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then follows the charge, in the first instance, against the patriarchs [after], the “signs and wonders,” after the receiving of the “lively oracles: To whom,” he says, “our fathers would not obey.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But concerning those, Ezekiel says that they are not “living;” as when he says, “And I gave you statutes that are not good.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is with reference to those that he says, “Living.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But thrust him from them, and in their hearts turned back to Egypt”—the place where they groaned, where they cried, whence they called upon God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And said unto Aaron, Make us gods which shall go before us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O the folly!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Make,” say they; “that they may go before us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Into Egypt.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how hard they were to tear away from the customs of Egypt!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What, not wait for him that brought thee out, but flee the benefit, and deny the Benefactor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And mark how insulting they are: “For as for this Moses,” they say:—“which brought us out of the land of Egypt” nowhere the name of God: instead of that, they ascribed all to Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where they ought to give thanks (to God), they bring Moses forward: where it was, to do as the Law bade them, they no longer make account of Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We know not what is become of him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he told them that he was going up to receive the Law: and they had not patience to wait forty days.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Make us gods”—they did not say, “a God.”—And yet one may well wonder at this, that they do not even know.—“And they made a calf in those days, and offered sacrifices unto the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their own hands” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 41): for which they ought to have hid their faces.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What wonder that ye know not Christ, seeing ye knew not Moses, and God Who was manifested by such wonders?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they not only knew Him not: they also insulted in another way, by their idol making.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then God turned, and gave them up to worship the host of heaven” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence these same “customs” date their origin, hence the sacrifices: they were themselves the first that made sacrifices to their idols!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that is why it is marked, “They made a calf in Horeb, and offered sacrifices to the idol:” seeing that, before this the name of sacrifice is nowhere mentioned, but only lively ordinances, and “lively oracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And rejoiced”—that is the reason for the feasts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Exod. xxxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As it is written in the Book of the Prophets”—and observe, he does not cite the text without a purpose, but shows by it that there is no need of sacrifices; saying: “Did ye offer slain beasts and sacrifice to Me?”—He lays an emphasis on this word (to Me?).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye cannot say that it was from sacrificing to Me, that ye proceeded to sacrifice to them:—“by the space of forty years:” and this too, “in the wilderness,” where He had most signally shown Himself their Protector.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yea, ye took up the tabernacle of Moloch, and the star of your god Remphan: images which ye made to worship them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The cause of sacrifices!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And I will carry you away beyond Babylon.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even the captivity, an impeachment of their wickedness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But a Tabernacle,” say you, “there was (the Tabernacle) ‘of Witness.’” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Yes,) this is why it was: that they should have God for Witness: this was all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “According to the fashion,” it says, “that was shown thee on the mount:” so that on the mount was the Original.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this Tabernacle, moreover, “in the wilderness,” was carried about, and not locally fixed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he calls it, “Tabernacle of witness:” i.e. (for witness) of the miracles, of the statutes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the reason why both it and those (the fathers) had no Temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As He had appointed, that spake unto Moses, that he should make it according to the fashion that he had seen.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, it was none other than He (Christ) that gave the fashion itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Until the days of David” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 45): and there was no temple!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet the Gentiles also had been driven out: for that is why he mentions this: “Whom God drave out,” he says, “before the face of our fathers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whom He drave out,” he says: and even then, no Temple!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And so many wonders, and no mention of a Temple!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that, although first there is a Tabernacle, yet nowhere a Temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Until the days of David,” he says: even David, and no Temple!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he sought to find favor before God” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 46): and built not:—so far was the Temple from being a great matter!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Solomon built Him an house.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They thought Solomon was great: but that he was not better than his father, nay not even equal to him, is manifest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Howbeit the Most High dwelleth not in temples made with hands; as saith the prophet, Heaven is My throne, and earth is My footstool.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, not even these are worthy of God, forasmuch as they are made, seeing they are creatures, the works of His hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he leads them on by little and little (showing) that not even these are to be mentioned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again the prophecy says openly, “What house will ye build Me?” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is the reason that at this point he speaks in the tone of invective (καταφορικὥς)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was his boldness of speech, when at the point to die: for in fact I think he knew that this was the case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye stiffnecked,” he says, “and uncircumcised in heart and ears.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This also is from the prophets: nothing is of himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye do always resist the Holy Ghost: as your fathers did, so do ye.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When it was not His will that sacrifices should be, ye sacrifice: when it is His will, then again ye do not sacrifice: when He would not give you commandments, ye drew them to you: when ye got them, ye neglected them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, when the Temple stood, ye worshipped idols: when it is His will to be worshipped without a Temple, ye do the opposite.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, he says not, “Ye resist God,” but, “the Spirit:” so far was he from knowing any difference between Them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, what is greater: “As your fathers did,” he says, “so do ye.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also did Christ (reproach them), forasmuch as they were always boasting much of their fathers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which of the prophets have not your fathers persecuted? and they have slain them which showed before of the coming of the Just One:” he still says, “the Just One,” wishing to check them: “of Whom ye have been now the betrayers and murderers”—two charges he lays against them—“who have received the Law by the disposition of Angels, and have not kept it.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How, “By the disposition of Angels?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some say (The Law), disposed by Angels; or, put into his hand by the Angel Who appeared to him in the bush; for was He man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No wonder that He who wrought those works, should also have wrought these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye slew them who preached of Him,” much more Himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows them disobedient both to God, and to Angels, and the Prophets, and the Spirit, and to all: as also Scripture saith elsewhere: “Lord, they have slain Thy Prophets, and thrown down Thine altars.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Kings xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They, then, stand up for the Law, and say, “He blasphemeth against Moses:” he shows, therefore, that it is they who blaspheme, and that (their blasphemy is not only against Moses, but) against God; shows that “they” from the very beginning have been doing this: that “they” have themselves destroyed their “customs,” that there is no need of these: that while accusing him, and saying that he opposed Moses, they themselves were opposing the Spirit: and not merely opposing, but with murder added to it: and that they had their enmity all along from the very beginning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou, that he shows them to be acting in opposition both to Moses and to all others, and not keeping the Law?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet Moses had said, “A Prophet shall the Lord raise up unto you: and the rest also told of this (Christ) that He would come: and the prophet again said, “What house will ye build Me?” and again, “Did ye offer to Me slain beasts and sacrifices” those “forty years?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Deut. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is the boldness of speech of a man bearing the Cross.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then also imitate this: though it be not a time of war, yet it is always the time for boldness of speech.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “I spake,” says one, “in Thy testimonies before kings, and was not ashamed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we chance to be among heathens, let us thus stop their mouths. without wrath, without harshness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hom. in 1 Cor. iv. §6; xxxiii. §4, 5; Col. xi. §2.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if we do it with wrath, it no longer seems to be the boldness (of one who is confident of his cause,) but passion: but if with gentleness, this is boldness indeed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in one and the same thing success and failure cannot possibly go together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The boldness is a success: the anger is a failure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, if we are to have boldness, we must be clean from wrath that none may impute our words to that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No matter how just your words may be, when you speak with anger, you ruin all: no matter how boldly you speak, how fairly reprove, or what not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See this man, how free from passion as he discourses to them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he did not abuse them: he did but remind them of the words of the Prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, to show you that it was not anger, at the very moment he was suffering evil at their hands, he prayed, saying, “Lay not to their charge this sin.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far was he from speaking these words in anger; no, he spake in grief and sorrow for their sakes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As indeed this is why it speaks of his appearance, that “they saw his face as it had been the face of an angel,” on purpose that they might believe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then be clean from wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Holy Spirit dwelleth not where wrath is: cursed is the wrathful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It cannot be that aught wholesome should approach, where wrath goes forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as in a storm at sea, great is the tumult, loud the clamor, and then would be no time for lessons of wisdom (φιλοσοφεἵν): so neither in wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the soul is to be in a condition either to say, or to be disciplined to, aught of philosophy, it must first be in the haven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not how, when we wish to converse on matters of serious import, we look out for places free from noise, where all is stillness, all calm, that we may not be put out and discomposed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if noise from without discomposes, much more disturbance from within.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whether one pray, to no purpose does he pray “with wrath and disputings:” (1 Tim. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8) whether he speak, he will only make himself ridiculous: whether he hold his peace, so again it will be even then: whether he eat, he is hurt even then: whether he drink, or whether he drink not; whether he sit, or stand, or walk; whether he sleep: for even in their dreams such fancies haunt them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what is there in such men that is not disagreeable?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Eyes unsightly, mouth distorted, limbs agitated and swollen, tongue foul and sparing no man, mind distraught, gestures uncomely: much to disgust.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the eyes of demoniacs, and those of drunkards and madmen; in what do they differ from each other?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not the whole madness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what though it be but for the moment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The madman too is possessed for the moment: but what is worse than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they are not ashamed at that excuse; “I knew not (saith one) what I said.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how came it that thou didst not know this, thou the rational man, thou that hast the gift of reason, on purpose that thou mayest not act the part of the creatures without reason, just like a wild horse, hurried away by rage and passion?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In truth, the very excuse is criminal.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thou oughtest to have known what thou saidst.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “It was the passion,” say you, “that spoke the words, not I.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How should it be that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For passion has no power, except it get it from you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You might as well say, “It was my hand that inflicted the wounds, not I.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What occasion, think you, most needs wrath? would you not say, war and battle?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But even then, if anything is done with wrath, the whole is spoiled and undone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of all men, those who fight had best not be enraged: of all men, those had best not be enraged, who want to hurt (τοὺς ὑβρίζοντας).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how is it possible to fight then? you will ask.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With reason, with self-command (ἐπιεικεί& 139·): since fighting is, to stand in opposition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not that even these (common) wars are regulated by definite law, and order, and times?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For wrath is nothing but an irrational impulse: and an irrational creature cannot possibly perform aught rational.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, the man here spoke such words, and did it without passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Elias said,” How long will ye halt on both your knees?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Kings xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21) and spake it not in passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Phinees slew, and did it without passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For passion suffers not a man to see, but, just as in a night-battle, it leads him, with eyes blindfolded and ears stopped up, where it will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us rid ourselves of this demon, at its first beginning let us quell it, let us put the sign of the Cross on our breast, as it were a curb.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wrath is a shameless dog: but let it learn to hear the law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If there be in a sheep-fold a dog so savage as not to obey the command of the shepherd, nor to know his voice, all is lost and ruined.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is kept along with the sheep: but if he makes a meal on the sheep, he is useless, and is put to death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he has learnt to obey thee, feed thy dog: he is useful when it is against the wolves, against robbers, and against the captain of the robbers that he barks, not against the sheep, not against friends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he does not obey he ruins all: if he learns not to mind thee, he destroys all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The mildness in thee let not wrath consume, but let it guard it, and feed it up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it will guard it, that it may feed in much security, if it destroy wicked and evil thoughts, if it chase away the devil from every side.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So is gentleness preserved, when evil works are nowhere admitted: so we become worthy of respect, when we learn not to be shameless.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For nothing renders a man so shameless, as an evil conscience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why are harlots without shame?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why are virgins shamefaced?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it not from their sin that the former, from their chastity that the latter, are such?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For nothing makes a person so shameless, as sin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And yet on the contrary,” say you, “it puts to shame.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes; him who condemns himself: but him that is past blushing, it renders even more reckless: for desperation makes daring.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For “the wicked,” saith the Scripture, “when he is come into the depths of evils, despiseth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Prov. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he that is shameless, will also be reckless, and he that is reckless, will be daring.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See in what way gentleness is destroyed, when evil thoughts gnaw at it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why there is such a dog, barking mightily: we have also sling and stone (ye know what I mean): we have also spear and enclosure and cattle-fold: let us guard our thoughts unhurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the dog be gentle (σαίνῃ) with the sheep, but savage against those without, and keep vigilant watch, this is the excellence of a dog: and, be he ever so famished, not to devour the sheep; be he ever so full, not to spare the wolves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such too is anger meant to be: however provoked, not to forsake gentleness; however at quiet, to be on the alert against evil thoughts: to acknowledge the friend, and not for any beating forsake him, and for all his caressing, to fly at the intruder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The devil uses caressing full oft: let the dog know at sight that he is an intruder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So also let us caress (σαίνωμεν) Virtue, though she put us to pain, and show our aversion to Vice, though she give us pleasure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not be worse than the dogs, which, even when whipped and throttled, do not desert their master: but if the stranger also feed them, even so they do hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There are times when anger is useful; but this is when it barks against strangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means it, “Whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It means, Stand not up in thine own quarrel, neither avenge thyself: if thou see another suffering deadly wrong, stretch out thy hand to help him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is no longer passion, when thou art clear of all feeling for thyself alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: David had gotten Saul into his power, and was not moved by passion, did not thrust the spear into him, the enemy he had in his power; but took his revenge upon the Devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Sam. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moses, when he saw a stranger doing an injury, even slew him (Exod. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22): but when one of his own people, he did not so: them that were brethren he would have reconciled; the others not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That “most meek” (Num. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 3) Moses, as Scripture witnesseth of him, see how he was roused!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not so, we: on the contrary, where we ought to show meekness, no wild beast so fierce as we: but where we ought to be roused, none so dull and sluggish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Hom. vi. de laud.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Pauli, ad fin.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On no occasion do we use our faculties to the purpose they were meant for: and therefore it is that our life is spent to no purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even in the case of implements; if one use them, one instead of other, all is spoilt: if one take his sword, and then, where he should use it and cut with it, uses only his hand, he does no good: again, where he should use his hand, by taking the sword in hand he spoils all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In like manner also the physician, if where he ought to cut, he cuts not, and where he ought not, he does cut, mars all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore, I beseech you, let us use the thing (τᾥ πράγματι) at its proper time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The proper time for anger is never, where we move in our own quarrel: but if it is our duty to correct others, then is the time to use it, that we may by force deliver others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Hom. in Matt. xvi. §7.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So shall we both be like unto God, always keeping a spirit free from wrath, and shall attain unto the good things that are to come, through the grace and loving-kindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, with whom, to the Father and the Holy Ghost together, be glory, dominion, and honor, now and evermore, world without end.
